h1Title: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disease driven by a defective skin barrier and a dysregulated type-2 immune response, characterised by intense itch, dry skin, and lichenified plaques. It affects approximately 230 million people worldwide and is the most prevalent non-communicable skin disease globally (GBD 2019). In the United States, 9.6 million children and 16.5 million adults have atopic dermatitis, representing prevalence rates of 13% and 7.3% respectively (IFSI 2019). It is the leading cause of skin disease burden and is strongly associated with anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbance. Moderate-to-severe disease is treated with topical corticosteroids and calcineurin inhibitors; the biologic dupilumab (targeting IL-4/IL-13 signalling) has transformed outcomes for adult and paediatric moderate-severe disease that fails topical therapy.

keyStats.prevalence: 230 million people worldwide (GBD 2019); 9.6 million US children (13%) and 16.5 million US adults (7.3%) with atopic dermatitis (IFSI 2019)
keyStats.demographics: Onset before age 5 in 70% of cases; higher prevalence in urban vs rural settings; Black children in the US have 1.7× higher prevalence than White children; no clear sex difference in childhood, slight female predominance in adults
keyStats.avgAge: Onset before 5 years in 70% of patients; adult-onset AD (after age 18) accounts for approximately 25% of cases; childhood disease remits by adulthood in 50-70% of mild cases
keyStats.globalCases: 230 million worldwide; disability-adjusted life-years from atopic dermatitis rank it as the leading non-communicable skin disease globally (GBD 2021)

definition: Atopic dermatitis (ICD-10: L20) is a chronic, relapsing-remitting inflammatory skin disease characterised pathologically by epidermal barrier dysfunction, Th2-skewed immune activation, and sensitisation to environmental allergens and staphylococcal antigens. Loss-of-function mutations in filaggrin (FLG, encoding a structural epidermal protein) are the single largest genetic risk factor, present in 30-40% of European patients with moderate-to-severe disease and causing deficient natural moisturising factor production, increased transepidermal water loss, and enhanced allergen penetration. The immune response is driven by Th2 cytokines IL-4 and IL-13, which suppress keratinocyte differentiation genes and the expression of filaggrin, claudin-1, and loricrin — creating a self-perpetuating cycle of barrier failure. IL-31 is the dominant pruritogen (itch mediator) and is co-secreted by Th2 cells and innate lymphoid cells type 2 (ILC2). Chronic scratching damages the barrier further, amplifying the cycle. Disease severity is classified as mild, moderate, or severe using validated scores — the Eczema Area and Severity Index (EASI), SCORAD, and Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure (POEM) — which guide treatment escalation. Atopic dermatitis is part of the atopic triad and predicts subsequent development of allergic rhinitis (30-40%) and asthma (25-35%).

typesClassification.description: Most common onset pattern; typically begins before age 2 with cheek and scalp involvement, later involving antecubital and popliteal fossae (flexural distribution). Severity peaks in the first 5 years; 50-70% of mild cases remit by adolescence. Associated with egg and milk food allergy in infancy.

Distribution shifts to the neck, face, and dorsal hands; lichenification (skin thickening) and prurigo nodules develop from chronic scratching. Facial disease causes significant psychosocial impact. Filaggrin mutations more prevalent in this persistent adult group.

Accounts for 20-30% of patients; clinical appearance identical to IgE-associated disease but allergy skin tests and serum IgE are normal. Predominantly female with later onset; inflammatory cytokine profile still Th2-dominant. Often responds equivalently to standard treatment.

A subtype or variant characterised by coin-shaped, well-defined, intensely itchy plaques predominantly on the limbs; associated with xerosis and secondary Staphylococcus aureus colonisation. May be confused with tinea corporis; skin scraping differentiates them.

EASI above 21 or POEM above 16 with inadequate response to optimised topical therapy and at least one course of systemic therapy. This group is eligible for biologic treatment (dupilumab, tralokinumab) or JAK inhibitors (abrocitinib, upadacitinib) per NICE, EMA, and FDA approval.

primarySymptoms: Intense pruritus (itch) that is persistent and often worse at night, causing sleep disruption, fatigue, and behavioural disturbance in children; the defining and most burdensome symptom, mediated primarily by IL-31.

Dry, scaly skin (xerosis) throughout the body — not just in affected patches — reflecting a systemic barrier defect beyond the visible lesions; aggravated by low humidity, frequent bathing with soap, and cold weather.
Erythematous (red), ill-defined, oozing patches and plaques in infants and young children — typically on the cheeks, forehead, scalp, and extensor surfaces.

Flexural lichenification — thickened, leathery skin with accentuated skin markings in the antecubital fossae, popliteal fossae, and wrists from chronic scratching, the hallmark distribution in older children and adults.
Excoriations — linear scratch marks, often with serous crusting, visible over active plaques; secondary impetiginisation with golden-yellow crusting indicates Staphylococcus aureus superinfection.
Bilateral periorbital darkening (atopic shiners), Dennie-Morgan lines (infraorbital folds), and conjunctival injection from chronic eye rubbing in allergen-sensitised patients.
Post-inflammatory hypo- or hyperpigmentation in darker skin tones — often more prominent than the active eczema and a significant cosmetic concern for patients of African or South Asian descent.
Keratosis pilaris — follicular horny plugs on the lateral arms and thighs, a common atopic marker not requiring treatment but indicating barrier dysfunction.
Nipple or genital eczema — often under-reported and severely affecting quality of life; managed with low-potency topical corticosteroids or tacrolimus applied briefly with specialist supervision.
earlyWarningSigns: Cheek redness and dryness in an infant under 6 months, especially if there is a family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever.
Persistently dry skin on the arms and legs of a toddler despite regular moisturising, with recurrent patches of redness over the elbows and knees.
Sudden onset of oozing, crusted patches in a child with known eczema — indicates Staphylococcus aureus superinfection requiring topical or systemic antibiotic therapy.
Focal skin vesicles clustered on an eczema plaque that suddenly worsen — suspect eczema herpeticum (herpes simplex superinfection), a medical emergency requiring urgent antiviral therapy.
emergencySigns: Eczema herpeticum — widespread punched-out erosions with clustered vesicles on eczematous skin, often with fever and malaise; requires urgent hospital assessment and IV or oral aciclovir to prevent dissemination to the CNS.

Erythroderma — generalised red, scaling, confluent inflammation covering over 90% of the body surface area with fever, rigors, and fluid imbalance; a dermatological emergency requiring hospital admission.
Anaphylaxis following introduction of a new food in a sensitised infant with eczema — swelling of face or throat, urticaria, wheezing; administer adrenaline and call emergency services.
Rapidly spreading skin infection with systemic sepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension) — Staphylococcal or streptococcal skin and soft-tissue infection in eczema can progress to septicaemia.

causes.cause: Filaggrin (FLG) gene mutations — the strongest single genetic cause
causes.description: Filaggrin is a structural protein essential for terminal keratinocyte differentiation, formation of the cornified envelope, and production of natural moisturising factor (NMF). FLG null mutations (R501X and 2282del4 are the most common European variants) are present in 30-40% of patients with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis and confer a 3-5-fold relative risk increase. Absent filaggrin increases transepidermal water loss, raises skin pH, and facilitates allergen and microbial penetration.

causes.cause: Th2-skewed immune dysregulation and IL-4/IL-13 pathway activation
causes.description: Atopic dermatitis is characterised by a Th2-predominant inflammatory milieu: IL-4 and IL-13 suppress barrier gene expression, promote IgE class-switching, and upregulate serum IgE. IL-31 causes pruritus directly by activating TRPV1 and TRPA1 channels on sensory nerve fibres. TSLP, IL-33, and IL-25 from damaged keratinocytes amplify type-2 immunity. Dupilumab, which blocks IL-4Rα (the shared receptor for IL-4 and IL-13), dramatically confirms this pathway as the primary therapeutic target.

causes.cause: Staphylococcus aureus skin colonisation and infection
causes.description: S. aureus colonises the skin of over 90% of patients with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis, compared with 5% of healthy skin. Staphylococcal superantigens directly activate T cells and mast cells, amplifying Th2 responses, disrupting the barrier, and triggering flares. S. aureus also suppresses the skin microbiome diversity that normally colonises healthy skin, perpetuating a feed-forward inflammatory loop.

Sensitisation to food allergens (egg, milk, peanut, wheat) through the disrupted skin barrier — transcutaneous sensitisation — drives 20-30% of childhood flares. Inhalant allergens including house dust mite, Alternaria mould, cat, and dog dander exacerbate disease in sensitised patients, particularly facial, neck, and hand eczema patterns characteristic of aeroallergen exposure.

Detergents, soaps, bubble baths, wool, synthetic fabrics, cigarette smoke, and extreme temperatures are potent non-allergic triggers that breach the barrier and provoke inflammation through direct keratinocyte damage. Low humidity environments — air-conditioned offices, heated winter rooms — dramatically accelerate transepidermal water loss and trigger flares.

riskFactors.description: One atopic parent confers 40-50% risk of atopic dermatitis; two atopic parents raise this to 60-80%. Filaggrin mutations alone double the risk of eczema persistence into adulthood. Polygenic risk score incorporating >50 loci now identifies high-risk neonates before first symptoms.

riskFactors.factor: Urban residence and reduced microbial diversity
riskFactors.description: Urban children have 2-3× higher atopic dermatitis prevalence than rural peers. The biodiversity hypothesis links reduced environmental microbial diversity to impaired Treg development and Th2 dominance. Farm children and those with early animal exposure have lower atopy rates.

Caesarean birth is associated with 20-25% higher atopic dermatitis risk in meta-analyses, attributed to reduced vertical microbiome transfer from the maternal birth canal, impacting early immune programming.

Antibiotic courses in the first year of life are associated with 40-70% higher atopic dermatitis risk in cohort studies, plausibly by disrupting gut microbiome diversity and Th1/Th2 balance during a critical immunological window.

riskFactors.factor: Prenatal and postnatal tobacco smoke exposure
riskFactors.description: Maternal smoking during pregnancy and passive smoke exposure in infancy are each associated with 20-30% higher atopic dermatitis risk. Smoke impairs skin barrier integrity and promotes IgE class-switching.

Black American children have 1.7× higher atopic dermatitis prevalence; severity is generally higher and follicular and papular patterns are more common. Post-inflammatory dyspigmentation is more marked and distressing in darker skin phototypes.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5: highest incidence and prevalence; onset before age 2 in 60% of cases. Up to 25% of children in high-income countries have some degree of eczema.
Black American children: 1.7× the prevalence of White children, with more severe disease and higher rates of secondary infection; access to biologic therapy is lower in this demographic.

Adult-onset atopic dermatitis: approximately 25% of cases first present after age 18, with a different phenotype — more facial, hand, and neck involvement; often initially misdiagnosed as contact dermatitis.
Healthcare workers: occupational hand eczema overlapping with atopic hand eczema, driven by repeated glove use and handwashing; a major occupational health burden.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical, based on the 2003 Williams / Hanifin & Rajka criteria, which require chronic or chronically relapsing pruritic dermatitis with a typical morphology and distribution, plus two or more minor features (personal or family history of atopy, IgE elevation, early age of onset, xerosis, Dennie-Morgan folds, etc.). No single diagnostic test is required or available. Disease severity scoring with validated tools — EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index), SCORAD, and POEM (patient-reported) — is essential for treatment selection and trial endpoints. Patch testing with a standard European or North American baseline series is performed when allergic contact dermatitis is suspected as a co-driver or alternative diagnosis, particularly in hand and facial eczema. Prick testing or specific serum IgE identifies contributing inhalant or food sensitisations, particularly relevant in children under 5 with severe disease or adults where aeroallergen avoidance may help. Skin swab for S. aureus culture and sensitivity guides antibiotic selection when signs of secondary infection are present. Skin biopsy is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty — to differentiate from cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL) presenting as eczema-like patches in adults, or psoriasis.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical assessment with validated severity scores (EASI, SCORAD, POEM)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies disease extent and severity to guide treatment escalation: mild (EASI 0-7), moderate (EASI 7-21), severe (EASI >21)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination of the full skin surface, with measurement of the affected area and intensity of each sign. POEM is a 7-question patient self-report completed in under 3 minutes. Results available immediately. Repeated at each visit to track response to treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies type-IV contact allergies that may co-exist with or mimic atopic dermatitis, particularly relevant for hand eczema, eyelid eczema, and treatment-refractory cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Approximately 30-130 allergen panels applied on adhesive discs to the upper back under occlusive patches. Left in place for 48 hours; readings at 48 and 72-96 hours. Avoid baths and heavy sweating during testing period.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin-prick testing and serum specific IgE
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies inhalant or food allergen sensitisation contributing to exacerbations, especially in children with severe early-onset disease and in adults with facial or periorbital pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Identical to the testing for allergic rhinitis. Must stop antihistamines 3-7 days beforehand. A positive result identifies sensitisation but must be interpreted in the clinical context — sensitisation does not always equal clinical relevance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies S. aureus colonisation vs. infection and antibiotic sensitivities to guide topical or systemic antibiotic selection in impetiginised eczema
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A moistened cotton swab rubbed over an inflamed area for 5-10 seconds, placed in transport medium. Results in 48-72 hours. S. aureus is detectable in most cultures from active eczema; MRSA requires altered antibiotic choice.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates atopic dermatitis from cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (mycosis fungoides), psoriasis, or contact dermatitis when diagnosis is clinically uncertain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy under local anaesthesia from a representative active lesion; single suture placed; dissolves or removed in 7-10 days. Histology shows spongiotic dermatitis with eosinophils in atopic dermatitis; no specific diagnostic marker.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows a stepwise approach per NICE NG190 (2021), AAD 2023, and ETFAD guidelines, escalating from emollients and topical corticosteroids through to biologics and JAK inhibitors. Emollients — the cornerstone of every treatment step — should be applied at least twice daily (500 g/week for adults, 250 g/week for children) to restore the skin barrier, reduce transepidermal water loss, and lower topical corticosteroid requirements. Step 1 (mild): regular emollient plus potency-matched topical corticosteroid (mild-strength for face, moderate-strength for body) applied to active areas until controlled; topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03-0.1% or pimecrolimus 1%) are steroid-sparing alternatives for the face and flexures. Step 2 (moderate): add proactive therapy — twice-weekly topical corticosteroid on previously affected sites to prevent recurrence; crisaborole ointment 2% (PDE4 inhibitor) for mild-moderate disease. Step 3 (severe uncontrolled): dupilumab 300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks (after a loading dose of 600 mg) is the transformative biologic targeting IL-4Rα; it reduces EASI by 50-75% at 16 weeks compared with placebo in adults and is FDA/NICE-approved for adults and children from age 6 months onwards. Tralokinumab (anti-IL-13) and lebrikizumab (anti-IL-13) are approved alternatives in adults. JAK inhibitors — abrocitinib 100-200 mg/day, upadacitinib 15-30 mg/day — provide faster onset (significant itch reduction within 2-4 weeks) and are approved for adults and adolescents (upadacitinib) with inadequate response to dupilumab or where dupilumab is contraindicated. Traditional systemic immunosuppressants (ciclosporin, methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate) remain options in healthcare systems without biologic access, though with less favourable benefit-risk profiles.

medicalTreatments.name: Emollients — leave-on moisturisers (aqueous cream, white soft paraffin, ceramide-containing formulations)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied liberally at least twice daily to all skin, not just active lesions. The most important long-term treatment in atopic dermatitis. Emollient selection should be guided by patient preference and skin type; ointments are most occlusive and effective for dry lichenified skin; lighter creams suit hot weather. Emollient therapy reduces flare frequency and topical corticosteroid consumption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2017: regular emollient use reduces flare frequency by 30-50% and time to next flare; number of flares reduced from approximately 2/year to 1/year in maintenance studies

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (TCS) — potency matched to site and age
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for active eczema flares. Mild (hydrocortisone 1%) for face and eyelids; moderate (clobetasone 0.05%) for flexures; potent (betamethasone valerate 0.1%) for body in adults; very potent (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) for lichenified plaques short-term. Apply once or twice daily for 2-4 weeks per flare; proactive twice-weekly dosing on healed lesions prevents recurrence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2004: TCS more effective than vehicle for all severity groups. Proactive twice-weekly TCS reduces flare rate by 50-70% over 24 weeks vs. as-needed use (PMID 18756519)

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (TCI) — tacrolimus 0.03%/0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing anti-inflammatory agents that inhibit calcineurin-mediated T-cell activation. Preferred for the face, eyelids, and genital area to avoid corticosteroid-induced atrophy. Tacrolimus 0.1% is most effective for adults; 0.03% used in children from age 2. Burning sensation on application is common initially; resolves within 1-2 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Head-to-head trials: tacrolimus 0.1% equivalent to moderate TCS for facial eczema; pimecrolimus equivalent to mild TCS. Long-term studies up to 3 years demonstrate safety; original black-box cancer warning largely retracted by accumulated post-marketing evidence.

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab (300 mg SC every 2 weeks; 600 mg loading dose)
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody targeting IL-4Rα, blocking both IL-4 and IL-13 signalling — the two dominant Th2 cytokines driving atopic dermatitis. Approved for adults, adolescents (12+), children (6+), and infants from 6 months with moderate-severe disease inadequately controlled by topical therapy. Also reduces co-existing asthma, nasal polyps, and allergic rhinitis symptoms. Adverse effects: conjunctivitis (15-20%), injection-site reactions; no immunosuppression-associated infection risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LIBERTY AD SOLO trials: EASI-75 (75% reduction) achieved in 38-44% on dupilumab vs. 9-12% placebo at week 16; EASI-50 in 60-70%. Itch NRS reduction from baseline approximately 50% vs. 15% placebo. Benefit maintained at 3 years in extension studies.

medicalTreatments.name: JAK inhibitors — upadacitinib 15-30 mg/day or abrocitinib 100-200 mg/day (adults/adolescents)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral JAK1-selective inhibitors with faster itch onset than dupilumab (significant itch relief within 2 weeks). Approved for adults with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis that fails topical therapy or dupilumab. Screening required before initiation: tuberculosis, hepatitis B/C, complete blood count, lipids. Contraindicated in pregnancy; increased VTE and cardiovascular risk warning from class-wide JAK inhibitor labelling.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: MEASURE-UP 1&2 (upadacitinib 30 mg): EASI-75 at week 16 in 73-80% vs. 16% placebo (PMID 33844979). Itch NRS50 in 60% at week 16. Superior EASI-75 response vs. dupilumab in head-to-head Heads Up trial at week 16.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciclosporin 2.5-5 mg/kg/day (short-term rescue or bridge to biologic)
medicalTreatments.description: A calcineurin inhibitor systemic immunosuppressant that reduces eczema severity rapidly (onset 2-4 weeks). Used as short-term rescue or while awaiting biologic funding approval. Maximum recommended duration 2 years due to nephrotoxicity and hypertension. Blood pressure and renal function monitored every 2-4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2020: ciclosporin reduces EASI by 55% at 12 weeks compared with placebo; used as the active comparator in most biologic trials

alternativeTreatments.name: Wet-wrap therapy (damp tubular bandage over topical corticosteroid)
alternativeTreatments.description: Damp bandages applied over diluted topical corticosteroid on active lesions enhance penetration and provide a physical barrier to scratching. Used for severe acute flares in children and adults. RCT evidence from Devillers and Oranje (2006) shows significant SCORAD reduction at 1 week compared with standard emollient alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Phototherapy — narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) 311 nm, 3 times per week
alternativeTreatments.description: Second-line systemic-equivalent therapy for patients with moderate-severe disease unable to use immunosuppressants or biologics. NB-UVB reduces Th2 inflammation and S. aureus colonisation. Cochrane 2016 evidence supports moderate benefit; cumulative UV exposure and access limitations are practical barriers.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist or allergist should be seen when eczema fails to respond to 2-4 weeks of appropriately potent topical corticosteroid, when disease is moderate-severe affecting sleep or daily function, when secondary infection is recurrent, when biologic therapy is being considered, or when the diagnosis is uncertain. Patch testing for contact allergen identification and allergy testing in children with possible food-triggered eczema require specialist input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a board-certified dermatologist with a dedicated eczema or inflammatory skin disease clinic, or an allergist with experience in atopic disease management. For biologic eligibility assessment, treatment centres with access to dupilumab funding pathways and monitoring infrastructure provide the best outcomes. Paediatric eczema is best managed at centres with integrated allergy and dermatology services.
hospitalCriteria: Biologic therapy initiation capability (dupilumab, tralokinumab) with nurse-led training and monitoring
Patch-testing suite with European or NACDG baseline allergen series
Paediatric dermatology with multi-disciplinary allergy input
Wet-wrap nurse-led clinic for acute severe childhood flares
keyFacilities: Specialist eczema / inflammatory skin disease clinics
Paediatric allergy and dermatology combined clinics
Patient support and education nurses (eczema nurses/nurse practitioners)
preventionStrategies: Apply emollient from birth in high-risk infants (positive family history of atopy): the BEEP trial found barrier-enhancing emollient from birth did not reduce AD incidence in the full cohort, but later sub-analyses support emollient use in high-risk infants with proven FLG mutations.
Avoid prenatal and postnatal tobacco smoke exposure — associated with 20-30% higher eczema risk in the child.
Breastfeed for at least 4-6 months where possible; early introduction of allergenic foods (peanut, egg) from 4-6 months per LEAP trial evidence reduces food allergy risk in at-risk infants.
Avoid unnecessary antibiotic courses in infancy — each course is associated with a 40-70% increase in eczema risk in observational studies.
Reduce indoor irritant exposure: use fragrance-free, pH-balanced detergents; keep humidity above 40% during winter heating season; minimise wool clothing against the skin.
lifestyleModifications: Apply emollient at least twice daily to all skin within 3 minutes of bathing (wet-skin application enhances penetration); use 250-500 g of emollient per week.
Bathe in lukewarm (not hot) water for 5-10 minutes using a soap substitute (Aqueous cream as a wash, bath oil, or syndet bar) — hot water and soap dramatically worsen barrier function.
Wear 100% cotton clothing next to the skin; avoid wool, synthetic fibres, and tight elastic waistbands that cause friction.
Keep fingernails short and smooth in children to reduce scratch-induced damage and secondary infection.
Maintain bedroom humidity at 40-50% with a humidifier in winter; wash bedding at 60°C weekly if house dust mite sensitive.

Manage stress with evidence-based techniques — cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and habit-reversal therapy specifically designed for itch-scratch cycles have been trialled with benefit in chronic eczema.

recommended: Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — meta-analysis supports modest reduction in eczema severity with fish oil supplementation, particularly in children

Probiotic-rich fermented foods or supplements during pregnancy and in infancy — Cochrane 2018 moderate-quality evidence shows 22% reduction in eczema incidence with prenatal plus postnatal Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG supplementation
Dietary diversity including a variety of vegetables, legumes, and whole grains to support microbiome diversity and Treg-mediated immune tolerance
Early introduction of peanut, egg, and other allergenic foods from 4-6 months in atopic infants per LEAP trial evidence
avoid: Confirmed food allergens identified by allergy testing — but food elimination should only be undertaken with positive allergy test results and dietitian supervision; elimination diets can cause nutritional deficiencies
Nickel-containing foods (chocolate, whole grains, legumes) in patients with proven systemic nickel sensitisation on patch testing — relevant for vesicular hand eczema
Artificial food additives (benzoates, azo dyes) may worsen eczema in a small subset of patients — a dietitian-supervised exclusion trial is appropriate if suspected
Alcohol — a known eczema flare trigger through vasodilation and inflammatory mediator release; even moderate intake can worsen nocturnal itch

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity aerobic exercise has systemic anti-inflammatory effects and improves mood, sleep, and quality of life, all of which benefit atopic dermatitis. Sweating can temporarily worsen itch; showering and applying emollient immediately after exercise reduces post-exercise flares. Swimming in chlorinated pools may irritate some patients — apply emollient before swimming and rinse thoroughly and moisturise after. Avoid exercise in very cold, dry air or extreme heat without appropriate emollient protection.
dailyManagement: Apply emollient to all skin at least twice daily — before topical corticosteroid in the morning and again at bedtime; the 'emollient first, wait 30 minutes, then apply steroid' protocol is widely used though concurrent application is also effective.
Use topical corticosteroid of the correct potency on active red areas only; do not use potent steroids on the face or in skin folds.
Check for signs of secondary infection every morning — golden-yellow crusting, rapidly expanding weeping, fever — and contact the GP or dermatology clinic promptly.
Keep a daily itch and sleep diary to monitor disease activity and report to the clinical team at reviews; POEM score can be completed weekly as a home monitoring tool.
Attend dupilumab or JAK inhibitor injection/monitoring appointments; report any new eye redness or blurred vision promptly if on dupilumab (conjunctivitis needs ophthalmology review in some cases).
Avoid known personal triggers (specific fabrics, detergents, foods, pets) and document triggers in a diary to identify patterns.
prognosis: Prognosis in atopic dermatitis is determined by severity, age of onset, and presence of filaggrin mutations. Approximately 50-70% of children with mild-moderate atopic dermatitis achieve remission by adolescence; severe childhood disease and filaggrin mutations predict persistence into adulthood in 50-60% of cases. Adult-onset atopic dermatitis tends to be more persistent and treatment-refractory. Dupilumab and JAK inhibitors have significantly improved the outlook for moderate-severe disease: at 16 weeks, EASI-75 is achieved in 70-80% of adults on upadacitinib 30 mg, and 38-44% on dupilumab — with maintained benefit at 3 years in extension studies. Untreated or inadequately treated severe atopic dermatitis carries a substantial psychological burden: anxiety and depression affect 30-40% of adults, and the risk of suicidal ideation is 44% higher than age-matched controls in recent register data. The atopic march — progression from eczema to rhinitis and then to asthma — occurs in 30-50% of patients with moderate-severe early-onset disease; allergen immunotherapy and early biologic treatment may interrupt this trajectory.
recoveryTimeline: Acute flares respond to topical corticosteroids within 3-7 days. Dupilumab onset: significant itch reduction within 2-4 weeks, EASI-75 at 16 weeks, with progressive improvement to 52 weeks. JAK inhibitors provide faster itch relief — NRS4 within 2 weeks for most patients. Emollient-based maintenance prevents relapse when used consistently but does not cure the underlying barrier defect. In children, reassessment of disease persistence should occur annually; many achieve substantial remission by adolescence with proactive topical therapy.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection (impetiginisation) — S. aureus or, less commonly, beta-haemolytic Streptococcus superinfection producing golden crusting and rapid worsening; treat with topical fusidic acid or oral flucloxacillin after swab.

Eczema herpeticum — disseminated herpes simplex superinfection producing clustered punched-out vesicles on eczematous skin with fever; requires urgent antiviral therapy (aciclovir 400 mg 5× daily or IV in severe cases); risk highest on the face.

Atopic march — development of allergic rhinitis in 30-40% and asthma in 25-35% of patients over years; children with early-onset severe eczema have the highest risk and should be monitored with annual spirometry from school age.

Corticosteroid-induced skin atrophy, telangiectasia, and striae from chronic overuse of potent or very potent topical corticosteroids on thin skin — prevented by potency matching, limiting application to active areas, and transitioning to steroid-sparing agents.

Psychological comorbidity — depression and anxiety affect 30-40% of adults with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis; chronic itch, sleep disruption, and cosmetic impact drive this burden. Routine mental health screening is recommended.

Contact sensitisation — atopic patients have a higher rate of developing type-IV contact allergies to common topical medicaments (neomycin, lanolin, fragrance) than the general population; patch testing identifies avoidable triggers.

supportResources.name: National Eczema Association (NEA)
supportResources.description: US patient organisation providing evidence-based eczema information, treatment guides, patient community, and access to the NEA Seal of Acceptance for skin-safe products.

supportResources.name: Eczema Society UK (ECZEMA)
supportResources.description: UK charity offering helplines, NHS treatment guides, published eczema management leaflets, and patient newsletter.

supportResources.name: NICE NG190 — Atopic Eczema in Under 12s (Patient Version)
supportResources.description: UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guideline for eczema management in children — publicly accessible patient and carer information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis presents with well-demarcated, thick, silver-scaled plaques predominantly on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, scalp), with a Koebner phenomenon on sites of trauma. Atopic dermatitis has ill-defined, weeping lesions in flexural distribution in children and adults; itch is more severe in eczema. Skin biopsy distinguishes the two if clinically uncertain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urticaria (Hives)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Urticaria produces transient (under 24 hours), raised, migrating wheals with central pallor and surrounding flare, which blanch on pressure — entirely different from the fixed, chronic, scaling patches of atopic dermatitis. Urticaria does not cause barrier damage, excoriation, or lichenification.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis is a nasal condition causing sneezing, rhinorrhoea, and congestion. It frequently coexists with eczema as part of the atopic triad but involves the nasal mucosa, not the skin. Both are diagnosed by allergy testing but have distinct organ-specific treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous T-Cell Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma) can present as chronic, scaling, pruritic patches mimicking atopic dermatitis or psoriasis in adults over 50. Distinguishing features include unusual fixed plaques that fail standard treatment, poikiloderma, and large atypical lymphocytes on biopsy. Skin biopsy with immunohistochemistry is diagnostic.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of the atopic triad; 25-35% of children with moderate-severe eczema develop asthma by school age through the atopic march.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in up to 60% of patients with atopic dermatitis; shares IL-4/IL-13 Th2 pathophysiology and responds to dupilumab in all three conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Major differential diagnosis sharing chronic pruritic skin disease features; different immune pathway (Th17 vs Th2) but increasing phenotypic overlap recognised in clinical practice.

relatedConditions.name: Urticaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are mast-cell and IgE-associated conditions that may share common sensitisation triggers; dupilumab is being studied in refractory chronic urticaria.

faqs.question: What is atopic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic skin condition causing intense itch, dry skin, and inflamed patches, driven by a defective skin barrier and an overactive immune response. It is the most common non-infectious skin disease globally, affecting approximately 230 million people. It commonly starts in childhood and often co-occurs with allergic rhinitis and asthma.

faqs.question: Is atopic dermatitis the same as eczema?
faqs.answer: Yes. Atopic dermatitis is the medical term for the most common form of eczema. The word eczema describes a pattern of skin inflammation, and atopic dermatitis is the specific type linked to atopy — IgE-mediated allergic sensitisation. Other forms of eczema include contact dermatitis, discoid eczema, and pompholyx.

faqs.question: What causes atopic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Atopic dermatitis results from defective skin barrier function — often due to mutations in the filaggrin gene — combined with an overactive Th2 immune response releasing IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31 (the itch cytokine). Environmental triggers include house dust mite, pet dander, skin irritants, and Staphylococcus aureus colonisation of the skin.

faqs.question: How is atopic dermatitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment uses a stepwise approach: generous emollient twice daily for all patients, then topical corticosteroids for active flares. Moderate-severe disease failing topical treatment is managed with dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 blocker) or JAK inhibitors. Dupilumab achieves 75% eczema area reduction in 38-44% of adults at 16 weeks.

faqs.question: Can atopic dermatitis be cured?
faqs.answer: 50-70% of children with mild-moderate disease achieve remission by adolescence. For persistent moderate-severe disease, dupilumab and JAK inhibitors control disease effectively but must be continued — they are not cures. Allergen immunotherapy may modify the underlying atopic immune programme for some patients.

faqs.question: What triggers atopic dermatitis flares?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include house dust mite, pet dander, pollen, skin irritants (soaps, detergents, wool), heat, sweating, stress, secondary Staphylococcus aureus skin infection, and in children under 5, food allergens (egg, milk, peanut). Identifying personal triggers with a diary and avoiding them reduces flare frequency.

faqs.question: Is atopic dermatitis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Atopic dermatitis is not contagious and cannot be spread to another person through contact. It is caused by genetics and immune system dysfunction, not by infection. Secondary bacterial infections like impetigo that can occur on eczema skin are potentially contagious, but eczema itself is not.

faqs.question: What is dupilumab and how does it treat eczema?
faqs.answer: Dupilumab is a biologic injection (every 2 weeks, given at home) that blocks IL-4Rα, the shared receptor for IL-4 and IL-13 — the main inflammation-driving cytokines in atopic dermatitis. It reduces itch, rash severity, and flare frequency without suppressing the whole immune system. It is approved from age 6 months for moderate-severe eczema.

faqs.question: What is the difference between eczema and psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Eczema causes ill-defined, weeping, intensely itchy patches in skin folds (elbows, knees, neck). Psoriasis causes well-defined, thick, silver-scaled plaques on extensor surfaces. Eczema is driven by Th2 immunity and often starts in childhood; psoriasis is driven by Th17 immunity and has no age preference. Biopsy distinguishes difficult cases.

faqs.question: Does food allergy cause eczema?
faqs.answer: In children under 5 with severe eczema, food allergens — particularly egg, milk, peanut, and wheat — trigger 20-30% of flares. Food elimination should only be undertaken after positive allergy testing under dietitian supervision, as unnecessary elimination diets can cause nutritional deficiency. Most eczema in older children and adults is not food-driven.

faqs.question: How often should I apply emollient for eczema?
faqs.answer: Emollient should be applied at least twice daily to all skin — not just affected areas — ideally within 3 minutes of bathing while the skin is still slightly damp. Adults typically use 500 g per week; children 250 g per week. Applying more emollient reduces the amount of topical steroid needed and the frequency of flares.

faqs.question: Can stress worsen atopic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Psychological stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and promotes mast-cell degranulation and Th2 cytokine release, worsening itch and barrier function. 30-40% of adults with atopic dermatitis have clinically significant anxiety or depression. Cognitive behavioural therapy and habit-reversal therapy targeting the itch-scratch cycle have shown benefit in clinical trials.

faqs.question: Is atopic dermatitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Strongly so. One atopic parent confers 40-50% risk; two atopic parents raise it to 60-80%. Filaggrin gene mutations — present in 30-40% of moderate-severe cases — are the single largest genetic risk factor. However, genetics alone do not determine disease; environmental factors including microbiome diversity, pollution, and allergen exposure also play major roles.

faqs.question: What is eczema herpeticum?
faqs.answer: Eczema herpeticum is herpes simplex virus infection disseminated across eczematous skin, causing clusters of punched-out vesicles and erosions with fever. It is a medical emergency requiring prompt antiviral treatment with oral or intravenous aciclovir. Risk is highest on facial eczema in children; it can be life-threatening if untreated.

faqs.question: Does atopic dermatitis cause asthma?
faqs.answer: In 25-35% of children with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis, asthma develops by school age — part of the atopic march. The same Th2 immune dysregulation drives both conditions. Annual respiratory review is recommended for children with significant eczema. Treating eczema effectively, including allergen immunotherapy, may reduce asthma risk.

metaDescription: Atopic dermatitis (eczema): chronic itchy inflamed skin from barrier defects and Th2 immunity. Emollients, steroids, dupilumab and JAK inhibitors reviewed.
